name: Transmedia Storytelling Theory (Jenkins)
core_claim: Henry Jenkins's transmedia storytelling theory (Convergence Culture, 2006; refined in the 2009 'Revenge of the Origami Unicorn' / seven-principles blog posts) makes a claim not about what drives enjoyment of a single work, but about what makes dispersed works belong to ONE story across media. Its founding move is that storytelling has 'become the art of world building': narrative identity is anchored not in a fixed plot or a single text but in a coherent STORYWORLD — a shared diegetic universe with stable geography, history, mythology, rules, and characters — that 'cannot be fully explored or exhausted within a single work or even a single medium.' A genuine transmedia story disperses integral elements systematically across multiple delivery channels (TV, film, comic, game, novel), where each instalment makes a distinct, valuable, and non-redundant contribution to the whole. The unifying glue Jenkins names is WORLDBUILDING plus CONTINUITY: 'the same story' is whatever sustains a coherent and plausible storyworld whose canon, internal logic, character ensemble, and mythology persist as you cross media. Jenkins's later seven principles formalise the tensions a transmedia property must manage: spreadability vs. drillability, continuity vs. multiplicity, immersion vs. extractability, worldbuilding, seriality, subjectivity, and performance. For HelpME2C the load-bearing idea is the inverse: if a story's identity across media lives in its WORLD (rules, mythology, setting-logic) and its CHARACTER ENSEMBLE and its THEMATIC concerns rather than in a literal plot, then 'these two shows are the same kind of story' can be operationalised at the level of world-archetype and thematic concern, which is precisely the cross-medium matching problem.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Primarily STRUCTURAL / PRODUCTION-aesthetic, and only secondarily descriptive. It is not an account of how a story FEELS (no affect, valence, or arousal dimension) nor a closed taxonomy of what stories are ABOUT topically. It theorises the architecture of narrative dispersal — how integral elements are distributed across media so that a coherent storyworld emerges — and the design principles (the seven tensions) that govern that dispersal. Where it touches the descriptive level, it is via WORLDBUILDING (the kind of universe: its setting, mythology, rules, scope) and via SUBJECTIVITY (whose perspective an extension adopts). It touches the experiential level only obliquely, through IMMERSION (entering the storyworld) and EXTRACTABILITY (taking elements out into one's life) — but treats these as design affordances of the property, not as measured viewer states. So it occupies a worldbuilding-and-narrative-architecture dimension distinct from the affect, motivation, and values frameworks elsewhere in this taxonomy.
key_dimensions: The framework offers two layers. LAYER 1 — the seven principles (Jenkins 2009), each a design tension: (1) SPREADABILITY vs. DRILLABILITY — content that ripples horizontally and accrues audience vs. content that rewards vertical descent into complexity by fewer, deeper fans. (2) CONTINUITY vs. MULTIPLICITY — a single coherent canon across media vs. sanctioned alternate versions / 'what-if' multiverse variants. (3) IMMERSION vs. EXTRACTABILITY — drawing the audience INTO the storyworld vs. letting them take pieces of it OUT into everyday life (merchandise, cosplay, quotation). (4) WORLDBUILDING — the construction of a coherent, internally consistent universe larger than any single plot. (5) SERIALITY — meaningful narrative chunks dispersed across instalments and platforms. (6) SUBJECTIVITY — exploring the world through additional characters' / perspectives' points of view. (7) PERFORMANCE — audience activities and fan productions that extend the narrative. LAYER 2 — the world-identity components implicit in the worldbuilding claim, which are the candidate vocabulary axes for HelpME2C: SETTING/WORLD-TYPE (the diegetic universe — its physics, geography, era), MYTHOLOGY/LORE (the internal rule-system and backstory), CHARACTER ENSEMBLE (the persistent cast that travels across media), CANONICITY (what counts as 'the real story'), and CROSS-MEDIUM EXTENSIBILITY (whether the world is rich enough to support extension). Geoffrey Long's MIT thesis (2007), working under Jenkins, adds operational sub-constructs — negative capability (deliberate gaps that invite extension), migratory cues, and hermeneutic vs. cultural codes — that sharpen the worldbuilding axis.
cross_cultural_validity: Largely UNTESTED cross-culturally as a formal matter, and Jenkins's exemplar base is heavily US/Western media-industry franchises (The Matrix, Star Wars, Pokémon, Lost, Heroes, Transformers, Marvel). The CORE intuition — that audiences recognise a shared world across media via continuity of setting, mythology, and characters — is plausibly broad: franchise-based worldbuilding is a global commercial form, and the anime/manga/light-novel/game 'media mix' that the cross-medium matching problem in HelpME2C cares about is itself a strongly non-Western (Japanese) transmedia tradition that PREDATES and partly inspired Jenkins's framing (Marc Steinberg, Anime's Media Mix, 2012, documents this lineage explicitly). That convergence is suggestive evidence that worldbuilding-as-narrative-identity is not purely Western. What is culturally specific is the CANONICITY politics (the producer-vs-fan authority struggle Jenkins foregrounds) and the PERFORMANCE/participatory-culture claims, which are bound to particular fan-cultural and legal/IP regimes; the East-Asian doujinshi tradition negotiates canon very differently from Western franchise gatekeeping. No systematic empirical cross-cultural validation of the seven principles exists. [uncertain]
empirical_grounding: Weak as a predictive or quantitative theory; this is humanistic media-studies built on case analysis, not on enjoyment, completion, or preference data. Convergence Culture and the seven-principles essays argue by close reading of franchise case studies (The Matrix, Star Wars, Survivor, American Idol) and ethnographic observation of fan communities, not by experiment or controlled measurement. There is NO validation tying the seven principles to viewer enjoyment, completion, or choice, and the framework has been explicitly criticised as conceptually vague and hard to falsify (e.g. the Graphy Online systematisation paper and several critiques note Jenkins's definitions are loose and contested). The empirical work that DOES exist is qualitative: fan-studies ethnography, production-studies case work (Long 2007 on the Jim Henson Company), and structural-pattern analyses (the PMC 'Structural patterns for transmedia storytelling' paper attempts a more formal computational treatment). To validate LLM-extracted world/theme dimensions against this framework you would not test the seven principles directly (they describe production strategy); you would test the WORLDBUILDING corollary — operationalise per-title world-type, mythology, ensemble-structure, and thematic-concern tags, then show that titles humans judge 'the same kind of story across media' cluster together on those tags and that such clustering predicts joint-viewing satisfaction. The framework supplies the conceptual warrant for world/theme-based matching; the predictive validation is entirely to-be-done.
operationalisability: Mixed, but with a usefully extractable core for the cross-medium problem. EXTRACTABLE from a synopsis (work-side, whole-work unit): WORLD-TYPE / setting-logic (a synopsis reliably reveals whether the universe is a magic system, a hard-SF future, a contemporary realist city, a post-apocalypse, etc.), broad MYTHOLOGY presence (does the premise rest on an internal lore/rule-system?), CHARACTER-ENSEMBLE shape (single protagonist vs. sprawling cast), and the THEMATIC concerns the world dramatises — which is exactly what a theme vocabulary captures. SUBJECTIVITY is partially extractable (synopses sometimes name a perspective shift). NOT extractable from a single synopsis: the genuinely transmedia-specific principles — SPREADABILITY/DRILLABILITY, CONTINUITY/MULTIPLICITY, EXTRACTABILITY, SERIALITY-across-media, and PERFORMANCE are properties of a FRANCHISE'S cross-platform deployment and its fan ecosystem, invisible in any one title's plot summary; they require franchise-graph metadata (does an IP span TV+film+game+manga?), fan-community signals, and release/canon data, not synopsis text. Natural extraction unit is the WHOLE WORK for world/theme tags, and the FRANCHISE/IP for the dispersal principles. Failure modes: (1) treating shared GENRE as shared WORLD (two unrelated magic-school shows share a world-archetype but not a storyworld — fine for matching, but the LLM must not assert literal connection); (2) mistaking a marketing 'cinematic universe' label for genuine narrative continuity; (3) conflating a single-medium serial with transmedia dispersal; (4) reading franchise breadth off a synopsis that does not mention it; (5) over-weighting plot summary and missing that world and theme, not plot, are what travel across media.
motivation_served: The framework maps most directly to IMMERSION/ESCAPISM (entering and inhabiting a coherent world — a hedonic and mood-regulation need) and to a distinctive COMPLETIONIST / mastery motive that drillability names: the pleasure of deep, vertical engagement with a world's complexity, lore-hunting, and connecting dispersed pieces. SUBJECTIVITY and the social/PERFORMANCE principles map to social-connection and identity-expression motives (fan participation, shared world-membership, IDENTITY through fandom). EXTRACTABILITY maps to identity expression carried into everyday life. For GROUP viewing the worldbuilding lens is unusually apt: two people can share a story not because they want the identical plot but because they both want to inhabit the SAME KIND OF WORLD (both want sprawling secondary-world fantasy; both want grounded near-future SF). World-archetype compatibility is a cleaner basis for a joint pick than topical overlap, because a shared inhabitable world satisfies both viewers' immersion need simultaneously; mismatch (one wants high-lore worldbuilding, the other finds it impenetrable) is exactly the drillability tension surfacing at the dyad level.
receiver_dependence: Mixed, with a clean split useful for a two-person recommender. The WORLD-side properties are largely ABSOLUTE work properties: a title either is or is not set in a richly built secondary world; it either has a deep mythology/rule-system or it does not; its ensemble is broad or narrow; its thematic concerns are what they are. These are stable, catalogue-able, and synopsis-estimable — the right granularity for cross-medium matching. The PARTICIPATORY and engagement-mode constructs are RELATIONAL: whether a world is experienced as 'spreadable' vs. 'drillable', whether a fan PERFORMS/extends it, and whether IMMERSION actually occurs depend on the specific viewer's appetite for lore-depth, completionism, and fandom participation — one viewer drills, the other skims the same property. CANONICITY-sensitivity (does it bother the viewer when extensions contradict?) is also viewer-relative. For HelpME2C: store world-type, mythology-depth, ensemble-shape, and thematic concerns as work-side catalogue tags; model lore-depth tolerance / drillability appetite and fandom-participation as a per-user relational layer, and treat world-archetype COMPATIBILITY between two users as the joint signal.
blind_spots: (1) It is a theory of FRANCHISE/PRODUCTION strategy, not of why an individual enjoys a single standalone work — most of the seven principles are simply inapplicable to a non-transmedia title, which is the majority of any catalogue. (2) NO affect or emotion dimension — it cannot say whether a world is comforting, tense, or devastating. (3) NO account of topical theme beyond 'worldbuilding' — it treats the world as the unit and is largely silent on the moral/identity/relationship concerns that other frameworks foreground. (4) Conceptual vagueness and low falsifiability — repeatedly criticised; the principles are heuristics, not measurable variables. (5) Commercial/franchise bias — built on big-IP exemplars, it under-theorises intimate, single-author, world-light drama (a character study with no 'world' to build) which it would simply have nothing to say about. (6) It privileges WORLD over CHARACTER INTERIORITY and over plot mechanics — it cannot distinguish two shows sharing a world-archetype but differing entirely in tone or depth. (7) Participatory-culture optimism — empirically contested degree of actual fan extension. (8) For matching: shared world-archetype is necessary-ish but far from sufficient for joint enjoyment; the framework alone would over-cluster on setting and miss tone, pacing, and values mismatches.
vocabulary_implications: Transmedia theory contributes a WORLD/SETTING-ARCHETYPE facet that is largely orthogonal to the current theme vocabulary — HelpME2C's existing slugs encode moral/relational/identity CONCERNS but have only scattered, genre-flavoured handles on the WORLD a story builds. Proposed work-side, synopsis-estimable additions, kept as a distinct 'world-archetype' facet (not mixed into topical themes): 'secondary-world' (a fully invented universe vs. our world), 'deep-mythology / lore-driven' (story rests on an extensive internal rule/history system — distinct from existing 'magic-system-cost' which is a THEME about price, whereas this is about world DEPTH), 'shared-universe / franchise-world' (title belongs to a multi-title connected universe — FRANCHISE-graph derived, NOT synopsis-only), 'ensemble-world' (broad persistent cast — reconcile with existing 'ensemble-mosaic'), and an ordinal 'worldbuilding-density' (low/medium/high) as a work-side prior. Reuse existing slugs that already encode world-archetypes: 'post-apocalyptic-survival', 'dystopian-society', 'alternate-history', 'first-contact', 'magic-system-cost', 'cosmic-horror', 'folk-horror' — these ARE world-types and validate the facet. Flag as NOT extractable from synopsis and requiring other data: the dispersal principles (spreadability/drillability, continuity/multiplicity, extractability, cross-media seriality, performance) — these need franchise/IP-graph metadata and fan-ecosystem signals; do not attempt to tag them from a single synopsis. Practically: add a controlled world-archetype facet that an LLM extracts from the synopsis, keep topical-theme tags separate, and treat 'same franchise / connected universe' as a metadata join rather than an LLM tag.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: It operates beside the topical theme vocabulary as a WORLD/SETTING facet, overlapping most with the Speculative group. It VALIDATES the speculative world-type slugs as legitimate world-archetypes that travel across media: 'post-apocalyptic-survival', 'dystopian-society', 'alternate-history', 'first-contact', 'technology-runs-amok', 'magic-system-cost', 'existential-horror', 'folk-horror', 'cosmic-horror' — these are exactly the 'kinds of world' worldbuilding theory says anchor cross-medium identity, and matching on them is well-founded. It also lightly validates 'ensemble-mosaic' (the ensemble-world / multi-subjectivity principle) and 'nonlinear-storytelling'/'time-loop' as world-logic markers. It suggests a MISALIGNMENT in that these world-type slugs are currently filed as topical 'themes' when they are really SETTING-ARCHETYPES — a different facet from moral/relational concerns — and would cluster more cleanly as a dedicated worldbuilding facet (the same separation conclusion reached by the texture-slug analyses elsewhere). GAPS it reveals: (a) no general 'secondary-world vs. real-world' distinction independent of genre flavour; (b) no 'worldbuilding-density / lore-depth' axis distinguishing a richly built universe from a thin backdrop sharing the same genre slug; (c) no 'shared-universe / franchise' relation at all, despite this being the single most direct cross-medium-matching signal the framework offers; (d) no handle on subjectivity/perspective-multiplicity as a structural property. It contributes little to the Relationship, Moral, and Identity groups, which it largely does not theorise.
key_sources: Jenkins, H. (2006). Convergence Culture: Where Old and New Media Collide. NYU Press (foundational; the worldbuilding-as-art-of-storytelling claim; print/paywalled). Jenkins, H. (2009). 'The Revenge of the Origami Unicorn: Seven Principles of Transmedia Storytelling' (Parts 1 & 2), Confessions of an Aca-Fan blog — FREE: http://henryjenkins.org/blog/2009/12/the_revenge_of_the_origami_uni.html and http://henryjenkins.org/blog/2009/12/revenge_of_the_origami_unicorn.html. Jenkins, H. (2007). 'Transmedia Storytelling 101' — FREE: http://henryjenkins.org/blog/2007/03/transmedia_storytelling_101.html. Long, G. (2007). Transmedia Storytelling: Business, Aesthetics and Production at the Jim Henson Company. MIT MS thesis (negative capability, migratory cues) — FREE PDF: https://cmsw.mit.edu/wp/wp-content/uploads/2016/10/147703402-Geoffrey-Long-Transmedia-Storytelling-Business-Aesthetics-and-Production-at-the-Jim-Henson-Company.pdf. Steinberg, M. (2012). Anime's Media Mix: Franchising Toys and Characters in Japan. Univ. of Minnesota Press (non-Western transmedia lineage; print/paywalled). Graphy Online (2016), 'A Systematization of the Concept of Transmedia' — FREE: https://www.graphyonline.com/archives/IJJMC/2016/IJJMC-120/ (collects critiques of Jenkins's vagueness). 'Structural patterns for transmedia storytelling', PMC — FREE: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6993987/. Practitioner summary of the seven principles — FREE: https://transmediajournalism.org/contexts/principles-of-transmedia/.
